items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: US-Iran MOU signed early — reserves running out in 4 weeks forced the hand
items.idea: The MOU was signed remotely ahead of schedule (expected Switzerland on Friday) because both sides were negotiating against a depletion clock, not a calendar. Trump said the quiet part: reserves run out in about four weeks. That is the pressure gauge. Hormuz reopening was the shared incentive. The deal opens frozen funds for Iran, kicks the nuclear file to Switzerland, and keeps missiles outside the immediate frame. A 60-day clock starts now. Iran's Foreign Ministry warned that continued Israeli attacks on Lebanon = breach of commitments.
items.angle: Market read: crude war premium bleeds off, inflation tail shrinks, rates can exhale, risk assets get a window. But this is proof-pricing now — show me tankers moving, show me reserve drain slowing, show me Lebanon not becoming the breach clause. Gold may stay bid through the rates channel even as oil falls.
items.tweetA: Trump said reserves run out in about four weeks. That is not diplomatic spin — that is the pressure gauge. The MOU came early because depletion math forced it. Now watch if tankers actually move, or if Lebanon becomes the breach clause that blows up the room. The paperwork is signed. The fire is not out.
items.tweetB: MOU signed. Crude war premium bleeds. Inflation tail shrinks. Fed exhales. But Iran kept missiles outside the frame, kept the nuclear file for Switzerland, and the 60-day clock means this expires in August. Relief valve not regime change. Everybody pricing in normality. Nobody pricing in what happens if the clause is triggered.

items.title: Cushing near tank bottom — US crude export collapse is coming regardless of MOU
items.idea: HFI Research flags a structural break that the MOU can not fix immediately: PADD 2 (Midwest) landlocked imports have dropped to 3.129M b/d vs. the normal 3.4M+ b/d. Cushing is already near tank bottom, so the Gulf coast can no longer pull barrels from inland to support exports. Canada does not have enough barrels to fill the gap. As PADD 3 inventories draw down and SPR flow rate slows, US crude exports will involuntarily drop — which forces overseas markets to tighten. Iran knows this same logistics timeline.
items.angle: The market is making a binary assumption: MOU signed = normality base case. HFI calls this wishful thinking. The physical supply chain is already broken in ways that do not heal on headline. Watch US weekly crude exports as the tell — if they drop while the MOU holds, the overseas tightening comes anyway.
items.tweetA: Everyone is watching the Hormuz MOU. Nobody is watching that Cushing is near tank bottom, PADD 2 land imports already fell 300k b/d, and US crude exports are about to involuntarily drop as PADD 3 draws. The MOU opens the legal door. The pipe does not have the barrels to walk through it. Iran knows this math.
items.tweetB: HFI Research: balancing on a knife edge is the most honest framing for oil right now. SPR flow rate slows + Cushing empty + PADD 2 imports down = US exports have to drop. That forces overseas tightening even if Hormuz reopens perfectly. The physical reality is lagging the diplomatic headline by weeks.

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: Warsh Washout — the Fed changed the price of the next hike without pulling the trigger
items.idea: The Fed held at 3.50-3.75% but did something more dangerous: it made hikes believable again. 9 officials now see at least one hike this year, 6 see at least two, only 1 sees a cut. The 2Y climbed to ~4.21%. Money markets fully priced a hike by October; 40% odds on July at the peak. This was not a policy move — it was a regime shift. The bar for a hike is now demonstrably lower and the bar for a cut is demonstrably higher. Warsh also opened a review of the Fed's $6.7T balance sheet, adding a second policy transmission lever back into market calculus. The hawkish turn was NOT just about energy — the committee signalled concern about labor, demand, AI capex investment, and productivity even as oil fell.
items.angle: Worst first-meeting SPX performance for a new Fed chair since Alan Greenspan. The important second-order: investors had positioned for a Warsh that would be dovish relative to Powell. That trade just got liquidated. Valuations need to earn their keep with a higher discount rate. The hurdle rate for every lazy duration, lazy multiple and lazy assumption just went up.
items.tweetA: Warsh did not hike. He did not need to. He changed the PRICE of the next hike. 2Y to 4.21%. October hike fully priced. 40% odds on July at the peak. The front end got punched in the mouth by a hold. That is the whole game — not 25bps, but the reaction function. Half the committee wants to tighten into an AI capex boom.
items.tweetB: A $6.7T balance sheet review. That is the quiet bomb in yesterday's FOMC. Everyone watching the dots. Warsh quietly opened the plumbing question. If policy is transmitted through both rates and liquidity, and you are already running thin pipes, even a modest valve turn changes pressure system-wide. This is not just about 25bps.

items.title: 9 of the 10 largest retail trading days EVER happened in the last month — sector correlations at confidence extremes
items.idea: Citadel Securities (Scott Rubner) data: May shattered previous activity records in cash equities. June is tracking 9% ABOVE May's record. Nine of the ten largest retail trading days ever observed on their platform occurred in just the last month, including seven during the first half of June alone. Separately, average S&P sector correlations have reached a market confidence extreme — the only comparable recent period was mid-2024, which preceded a 9% correction caused by the yen revaluation / Nikkei flash crash. The SPX 10-week MA has remained above the 40-week MA for 52 consecutive weeks.
items.angle: Retail is all-in at peak confidence, peak vol suppression, and peak correlation. This is the textbook setup: extreme crowding + extreme confidence + extreme complacency. Trend is intact but the crowding is extraordinary. These regimes resolve violently or they extend into melt-up. The Warsh washout arrived at exactly the wrong moment for this positioning.
items.tweetA: Nine of the ten largest retail trading days EVER occurred in the last month. June is tracking 9% above May's already-record volumes. The last time sector correlations hit this confidence extreme was mid-2024, right before a 9% correction. Retail is all-in at exactly the moment Warsh just changed the game.
items.tweetB: SPX has been in primary trend for 52 consecutive weeks. That deserves the benefit of the doubt. But the crowding data is extraordinary. Retail at record activity, sector correlations at extreme confidence readings, and now a hawkish FOMC that raised the hurdle rate. Melt-up or snap — this is not a boring setup. $SPY $QQQ

charts.desc: FOMC dot plot screenshot (Nick Timiraos), 10Y-2Y spread narrowing to flattest since Apr25, retail trading activity surge, sector correlation chart showing confidence extreme, SPX 52-week MA trend

items.title: Anthropic Blip 2.0 — day 6, longer than Sam Altman firing, 10-trillion-parameter models banned by US government
items.idea: Anthropic's Mythos and Fable models were taken offline by the US government last Friday — day 6 as of this writing, already 3x longer than Blip 1.0 (Sam Altman firing took one weekend). These are 10+ trillion parameter models, vs. the 1-2 trillion typical at OpenAI and Google. The US government concern: the models can scan 40-50 years of code and surface deep cybersecurity vulnerabilities — dual-use power. The Anthropic ban is now stirring concerns at OpenAI and beyond about a crackdown on foreign AI talent. China open source benefits in the near term. OpenAI burned $3.7B in Q1 2026. Elon closed a $60B acquisition of Cursor (AI coding leader) and is building his own mega-LLM.
items.angle: The permanent new risk premium for frontier AI is now established. Every AI IPO valuation now carries a government-action discount rate. The Blip is not a one-off — it is the new ceiling question. And the irony: the US government action designed to protect from China is accelerating China's open-source AI advantage.
items.tweetA: Anthropic's Mythos and Fable are offline — day 6. Blip 1.0 (Sam Altman firing) took a weekend. These are 10-trillion-parameter models. The dual-use fear: same capability that finds vulnerabilities in 50 years of code can also secure them. The US is fighting its own best AI company and China's open source fills the gap. The timing could not be worse.
items.tweetB: Anthropic models banned. OpenAI burns $3.7B in Q1. Elon pays $60B for Cursor. Rivals racing to 1.5T+ parameters. The Blip 2.0 is the first proof that government can unilaterally remove frontier AI from the market. Every AI IPO now has a new risk factor. This is permanent uncertainty, not a blip.

items.title: Qualcomm reportedly in talks to acquire AI chip company Tenstorrent for up to $10 billion
items.idea: TheStreet reports Qualcomm is in talks to acquire Tenstorrent — the RISC-V based AI chip startup — in an up to $10 billion deal. This is a direct play on the AI inference and edge compute architecture war. Tenstorrent has been building an open-source RISC-V alternative to CUDA-dependent architectures. A Qualcomm acquisition at $10B would signal that established chip makers are paying serious money to compete with Nvidia on the inference and edge layers.
items.angle: The compute architecture war just escalated. RISC-V vs. CUDA. If Qualcomm closes this at $10B, they have a credible edge AI and inference platform for the post-training inference market. Every 'AI chip' investment thesis just got more complicated. $QCOM $NVDA
items.tweetA: Qualcomm reportedly buying Tenstorrent for $10B. Tenstorrent was building RISC-V based AI chips — open architecture alternative to CUDA. At $10B, Qualcomm is saying: we are not going to let NVDA own inference forever. The compute architecture war just became a bidding war. $QCOM $NVDA
items.tweetB: A $10B AI chip acquisition. Qualcomm + Tenstorrent = edge AI inference contender. This is the hedge trade against CUDA lock-in. If RISC-V scales for inference, the moat around Nvidia's ecosystem starts looking less permanent. Watch whether AMD makes a counter or defensive move. $QCOM $AMD $NVDA

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: Market right on rates for 24 hours — wrong on the follow-through if the consumer starts tightening itself
items.idea: Dark Side pushes back on the consensus Warsh read: the bigger macro risk is not inflation but the consumer running out of oxygen while the Fed watches smoke. Personal saving rate fell to 2.6% in April — the cushion is thin. Goldman's work suggests US spending headwinds have not yet fully materialized, especially as the tax refund boost fades into H2 2026. Lower oil helps the inflation headline but also signals softer future demand — if the energy shock already dented household real cash flows, the drop in oil prices is a warning, not a relief. The rates market may be right for the first 24 hours but wrong on follow-through.
items.angle: This is the classic Fed policy error trap: tightening into a demand slowdown that has not yet shown up in the data. If the consumer starts doing the Fed's tightening for it, the Fed gets credit for what was already happening — and then has to pivot sharply. The 2.6% saving rate is the number to watch.
items.tweetA: The rates market is right on Warsh for 24 hours. Wrong on the follow-through. Personal saving rate: 2.6%. Tax refund boost fading into H2. Goldman flags spending headwinds not yet materialized. The Fed is fighting inflation smoke while the consumer fire is quietly running out of oxygen. Two tightenings don't mix.
items.tweetB: Lower oil in an inflation fight looks like relief. Lower oil in a demand slowdown is a warning signal. The consumer may already be adjusting real spending before the Fed sees it in the data. If that's happening, Warsh wins by doing nothing — and the market will then have to un-price October hike odds very quickly.

items.title: Fed dot plot: core PCE raised to 3.3%, rates higher through 2027, inflation target not until 2028
items.idea: The FOMC kept rates at 3.50-3.75% unanimously. The dot plot update raised the median 2026 funds rate projection from 3.4% to 3.8%. Core PCE forecast for 2026 was raised from 2.7% to 3.3%. The Fed's official projection now requires rates to stay higher through 2027 to get inflation back to 2% by 2028. The committee sees continued inflation pressure from energy supply shocks. Labor market characterized as stable with job gains matching workforce growth. Economy growing at solid pace despite Middle East conflict uncertainty.
items.angle: The Fed just extended its own timeline by a full year. This is a 3-year inflation fight projected on the official record — not a 3-quarter pivot story. Everything with duration has to reprice to reflect this. The 2026 core PCE number at 3.3% is the one that should shock: that's almost double the target, a full year from now.
items.tweetA: Fed dot plot: core PCE 2026 revised from 2.7% to 3.3%. Median 2026 rate from 3.4% to 3.8%. The official projection now requires higher rates through 2027 and does not hit target until 2028. That is a 3-year inflation fight on record. The market was priced for a 3-quarter pivot story. These two timelines are incompatible.
items.tweetB: 2026 core PCE at 3.3% — that is the number nobody is talking about. Almost double the target, 18 months from now, in the official Fed forecast. The only path to a cut before 2028 is data coming in sharply below that projection. The bar for relief just moved a year further away.

items.title: Worst first-meeting SPX performance since Greenspan — Warsh announces 5 institutional task forces
items.idea: SPX fell 1.2%, Nasdaq -1.3%, Dow -1.0%, RUT -0.7% on the first FOMC meeting under new chair Kevin Warsh — the worst first-meeting performance for a Fed chair since Alan Greenspan. Warsh emphasized the institution is going back to first principles and announced five simultaneous task forces with recommendations due by year end: 1) communications, 2) the $6.7T balance sheet, 3) use and reliance on existing data sources, 4) on productivity and jobs, and 5) on the inflation framework. Warsh notably did not submit his own dot projection — first chair to skip since the modern era. May pending home sales rose 3.8%, fourth consecutive gain. Short end yields jumped; long end more subdued.
items.angle: Five task forces is an institutional audit, not tweaks. Warsh is rebuilding the Fed's operating model simultaneously with running the tightest stance in a generation. The data task force is particularly interesting — it could change how the Fed measures and responds to inflation. The fact that he did not submit his own dot is the most significant signal: maximum optionality preserved.
items.tweetA: Warsh launched five simultaneous task forces — communications, balance sheet, data, productivity/jobs, inflation framework — and did not submit his own dot. That is the first Fed chair to skip the forecast since the modern era. He is rebuilding the institution from the inside while simultaneously running the most hawkish stance in years. That combination is unprecedented.
items.tweetB: Worst first-meeting SPX performance since Greenspan. May pending home sales +3.8%, fourth straight gain, consumer holding. But Warsh just changed the weather: hike by October fully priced, July at 40%. The old Warsh trade — dovish surprise, cuts coming back — just got liquidated. The market mis-read the man.

items.title: Pre-FOMC decision tree: MOVE 69 vs realized 82, equity vol rich vs rate vol — the mismatch was the trade
items.idea: Princeton Chen's pre-FOMC playbook (written before 2pm ET, before the decision): MOVE at 69 vs. realized rate vol of 82 — rate vol was genuinely cheap going in. VIX at 19 vs. realized equity vol of 15 — equity vol was already expensive relative to rates. Recommended TLT straddle (long rate vol, not a rate direction bet) as the cheap insurance. 2Y/10Y/30Y going in: 4.09 / 4.50 / ~5.0%. Key edge: read the DOT DISPERSION not the median — a calm median can hide a hawkish split. The Warsh-omitting-his-own-dot tail was identified as the cheapest convex outcome.
items.angle: This is the vol arbitrage play between asset classes: buy cheap vol where it is mispriced. Pre-FOMC rate vol was cheaper than realized moves. Post-FOMC equity vol is rich. The next iteration of this trade: if rate vol reprices higher and equity vol mean-reverts lower, the cross-asset vol spread compresses. Watch MOVE vs VIX.
items.tweetA: Before yesterday's FOMC: MOVE at 69, realized rate vol at 82. Rate vol was cheaper than what was already happening. VIX at 19, realized equity vol at 15. The mismatch was the trade. Cheap rate insurance, expensive equity insurance. Read the dispersion of the dots, not the median. That edge hit.
items.tweetB: The pre-FOMC read that mattered: Warsh omitting his own dot was the cheapest convex tail outcome. It hit. Now the question is what the dispersion looks like next meeting — and whether MOVE continues to lag realized vol. If it does, the same trade reloads. Watch 2s30s, not SPX, for the signal. $TLT $MOVE

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (decision tree graphic, dot dispersion analysis)

items.title: O*NET for AI R&D — 60-task taxonomy to measure intelligence explosion proximity
items.idea: Epoch AI proposes a first taxonomy of 60+ tasks spanning the full AI R&D workflow at a frontier lab, organized into six categories (ideation, experimentation, coding, running/monitoring, evaluation, communication), each task rated 0-5 on how much current AI automates it. Current AI timeline forecasting relies on proxies (compute curves, METR time horizons) that miss the actual tasks that matter. The O*NET framework allows: tracking fraction of tasks automated over time, interpreting benchmark results against what they cover, designing surveys of researcher uplift, and building a leading indicator for when AI research automation crosses the intelligence-explosion threshold.
items.angle: This is the first direct measurement framework for the question everyone actually cares about. When AI can automate AI research — specifically the tasks requiring taste (what experiment to run next, how to coordinate across 5 projects with unclear success criteria) — that is the inflection. This taxonomy makes that measurable for the first time.
items.tweetA: Epoch AI built an O*NET for AI research — 60+ tasks at frontier labs rated on how much current AI automates them. This is the first granular map of what automating AI R&D actually means. Until now everyone was extrapolating proxies. Can AI decide what experiment to run next? Can it coordinate 5 projects with no clear success criterion? Now we can measure it.
items.tweetB: The problem with AI timeline forecasting: we extrapolate what is easy to measure (compute, benchmark scores) not what we actually care about (which R&D tasks are automated). Epoch AI's O*NET taxonomy maps 60+ specific tasks. When those start hitting 4s and 5s on the automation scale, that is the leading indicator. This is the framework that matters.

charts.desc: Taxonomy table inside article — worth opening

items.title: Anthropic's Mythos + Fable: 10T parameters, 5x larger than current frontier — the technical core of the Blip
items.idea: Anthropic's Mythos and Fable are 10+ trillion parameter models — vs. the 1-2 trillion typical at OpenAI and Google. The US government's fear: this scale enables scanning decades of legacy code to surface deep cybersecurity vulnerabilities at unprecedented speed. The same capability that scares the DoD is the capability that could secure critical infrastructure, with the right protocols built in cooperation with governments. Rivals are not standing still: OpenAI, Google, and Chinese open-source models are all racing toward 1.5T+ parameters. Elon's xAI, newly fortified with the $60B Cursor acquisition, is headed the same direction.
items.angle: The 10T threshold is the line that triggered regulatory response. This is now a permanent category of risk for frontier AI companies. Every model training run above a certain parameter count will require pre-clearance or risk a Blip. Paradoxically, this drives the open-source advantage even harder — because open-source models cannot be taken offline.
items.tweetA: 10 trillion parameters. That is Mythos and Fable vs. 1-2T for the rest of the frontier. The government is scared because the same capability that finds vulnerabilities in 50 years of code can also exploit them. The Blip 2.0 is the first proof that scale = regulatory risk. Rivals are heading to the same threshold fast.
items.tweetB: The geopolitical irony: US national security action banning Anthropic's models is accelerating China's open-source advantage. Chinese models cannot be taken offline by the US government. The Blip 2.0 is the best thing that happened to DeepSeek and Qwen this week. Intended protection, unintended consequence.

items.title: Memory costs exceed 50% of smartphone hardware bill — Moore's Law suspended through 2030 for AI devices
items.idea: Nothing's CEO reports memory costs now exceed 50% of a smartphone's hardware bill. This is the first AI tech wave in history without Moore's Law cost-decline tailwinds — consumer hardware prices are increasing 20-40%+ on laptops, smartphones, and gadgets. AI device budgets add $500-$2,000+ on top of existing device spending ($1,000+ laptop + $500+ phone). Parekh argues Moore's Law benefits for mainstream consumer hardware are suspended through 2030, especially for AI-driven devices. Only Apple has supply chain insulation from this pressure.
items.angle: This inverts the standard tech adoption curve assumption. Previous waves (PC, mobile, internet) rode falling costs into mass adoption. This AI hardware wave rides rising costs. Adoption will be slower, more stratified, and more concentrated around companies that can absorb the supply chain risk. $AAPL uniquely positioned.
items.tweetA: Memory costs now exceed 50% of a smartphone's hardware bill. This is why the AI device wave is different from every prior tech wave — there are no Moore's Law cost tailwinds. $500-$2,000 additional for AI wearables on top of existing device budgets. Only Apple has supply chain insulation. Everyone else is pricing in wishes.
items.tweetB: Every major tech wave rode declining hardware costs into mass adoption. AI hardware is the first wave riding RISING costs. Memory up 50% of BOM. 20-40% price increases already on consumer devices. Moore's Law suspended through 2030. If adoption curves normalize from this base, the total addressable market math changes fundamentally.

items.title: Android 17 ships Gemini Omni to mainstream phones — frontier multimodal AI reaching billions
items.idea: Android 17 launched on Pixel today and will roll out across hundreds of OEM partners globally. It carries Gemini Omni — Google's latest multimodal model with image and music generation capabilities — now reaching mainstream Android users without a premium tier. Apple is simultaneously partnering with Google on Gemini. This is the consumer AI distribution event: world-class multimodal models (image, voice, music generation) reaching billions of mainstream consumers via OEM rollout over the next few months, not just early adopters.
items.angle: The adoption curve debate is becoming empirical. When a billion Android + billion iPhone users have access to Gemini Omni and Apple intelligence this fall, the question shifts from will consumers use AI to what do they actually do with it. Google is arguably the most interesting consumer AI company at scale given its OEM distribution reach.
items.tweetA: Android 17 just shipped with Gemini Omni — multimodal (image + music generation) — to mainstream phones via hundreds of OEM partners globally. This is not a demo for early adopters. This is frontier AI reaching billions of consumers in the next few months. The consumer adoption curve debate is about to become empirical.
items.tweetB: Apple intelligence this fall. Google Gemini Omni on Android 17 today. Both shipping frontier multimodal AI to mainstream consumers simultaneously — not power users, not paid tiers, mainstream. When a billion Android and a billion iPhone users have on-device AI agents, the 'will consumers adopt AI' question is answered. $GOOGL $AAPL

items.title: Snap 'Specs' at $2,195 — Vision Pro-class AR glasses with 1B users and a 90% vote-controlled CEO who needs a friendly buyer
items.idea: Snap unveiled Specs — Apple Vision Pro-class functionality in a glasses form factor, no external pucks required, at $2,195. Available for pre-order, shipping this fall. The company is down 30%+ this year at a ~$9B market cap with a billion social users and over a dozen years of AI smart glasses IP. 90%+ of votes are controlled by Evan Spiegel through 10-to-1 super-votes, meaning any acquisition must be friendly. Parekh floats Meta, Apple, Google, Amazon as potential acquirers. The 'Glasshole' societal concern returns — recording, recognition, privacy.
items.angle: At $9B with a billion users and 12 years of wearables IP, Snap looks like a call option on AI glasses at a potentially attractive entry. The constraint is Spiegel's voting control — only a friendly deal works. Apple + Snap integration would be extraordinary for AI wearables roadmap. $SNAP
items.tweetA: Snap is trading at $9B with a billion users, 12 years of smart glasses IP, and just launched Vision Pro-class AR glasses at $2,195 called Specs. Stock down 30% this year. 90% of votes are Spiegel's — friendly only. At some point this looks like a call option on AI wearables that a larger platform can afford to buy. $SNAP
items.tweetB: Snap's Specs: Vision Pro functionality. Glasses form. $2,195. No puck. Shipping fall 2026. Memory cost pressure means this should be $500-800 cheaper when supply chains normalize. The platform question for AR is who owns your face. Apple, Google, Meta, Snap — this is the race that matters most for the next decade of computing. $SNAP

items.title: HFT economics in 2026: Citadel Securities $9.7B revenue, roughly $1M net profit per employee per quarter
items.idea: Citadel Securities booked $9.7B in net trading revenue in 2024, a 55% increase over the prior year, and more than doubled net income to $4.2B. In Q1 2025 alone it added $3.4B in net trading revenue, up 45% year-over-year. With a relatively small headcount, this works out to roughly $1M of net profit per employee per quarter. This is why the HFT hiring funnel is as narrow as it is — the firms are dividing an enormous revenue pool among a small number of people and are exacting about who stands in the room.
items.angle: The $1M/employee/quarter number explains everything about HFT culture, pay, hiring selectivity, and retention. At this productivity level, one wrong hire is extraordinarily costly and one extraordinary hire is extraordinarily valuable. The firms are not being precious — they are being rational about variance.
items.tweetA: Citadel Securities: $9.7B net trading revenue in 2024, +55%. Net income more than doubled to $4.2B. Rough math: $1M net profit per employee per quarter. That is why the HFT hiring funnel is that narrow — they are dividing an enormous pool among very few people and they can afford to wait for exactly the right candidate.
items.tweetB: The reason HFT pays 5-10x most finance roles is not prestige — it is that the marginal employee at a top HFT shop adds and destroys enormous economic value. $1M net profit per employee per quarter at Citadel Securities. The selection process is that intense because the stakes are that high. $9.7B annual revenue from a small team.

items.title: Georgia redistricting killed by its own Speaker — 2028 House control now hinges on next map fight
items.idea: House Speaker Jon Burns sent a letter rejecting Gov. Kemp's redistricting request hours before the June 17 special session opened — a procedural kill switch. The current Republican House majority is 220-215 (only 3 seats of margin). One or more additional GOP-leaning seats in Georgia would meaningfully offset that exposure ahead of 2026 midterms and 2028. The Supreme Court VRA ruling changed the legal ceiling but voting-rights plaintiffs can still sue on racial gerrymandering and process defects. Democrats via Forward Majority are planning a $30M spend on state legislative races specifically tied to 2028 map control. A rushed map creates a weaker litigation record.
items.angle: The pause is strategic optionality, not surrender. Republicans get time to build a cleaner map with better litigation armor. Democrats spend $30M down-ballot to win the legislative seats that draw the 2028 maps. This is a multi-year board game and the Georgia special session was one move. Watch the 2026 Georgia state house races as the real bellwether.
items.tweetA: House Republican majority: 220-215. Three seats of margin. Georgia redistricting could add more. The Speaker just blocked it — not because they don't want the seats, but because a rushed map gives challengers the cleanest possible litigation record. This is strategic optionality. The 2028 map fight is still coming, just better armed.
items.tweetB: Democrats are spending $30M on state legislative races specifically to control 2028 congressional maps. Republicans are delaying a Georgia redistricting push to build a better litigation record. Both sides know the calculus: 3-seat House margin + weakened VRA = marginal maps have enormous value. The real fight starts in November.

items.title: MegaETH MOSS: smart approvals + agent permissioning — the wallet architecture that makes onchain AI agents safe
items.idea: MegaETH launched MOSS, a self-custodial smart account system that gives users one wallet across all apps on the chain with embedded-grade usability. The key feature is smart approvals: per-app sub-wallets with user-defined permissions — which contracts an app can call, how much it can spend, when access expires. If a compromised app can only access the $100 you scoped to it, phishing becomes seriously minus-EV. Additionally, Yang and Rivers hinted at a permissioning language expressive enough to delegate transactions to untrusted agents safely — this is the primitive for autonomous AI agents operating in DeFi.
items.angle: The agent permissioning angle is the most forward-looking piece. AI agents interacting with DeFi require trustless permission delegation — define scope, define limits, auto-revoke on session end. MOSS is building this architecture now, before the agents exist at scale. First-mover on the safe-agent infrastructure layer in crypto.
items.tweetA: MegaETH's MOSS: one smart contract wallet, all apps, user-defined spending limits per app. If a compromised app can only touch the $100 you scoped to it, phishing becomes minus-EV. This is the wallet architecture that makes DeFi actually safe to use. Not just UX — security model change.
items.tweetB: The most interesting part of MOSS is the agent permissioning layer. Define what an untrusted AI agent can call, how much it can spend, when access expires. This is the primitive that makes autonomous crypto agents possible without catastrophic counterparty risk. MegaETH is building the infrastructure before the agents arrive.

items.title: Illinois signs Digital Asset Tax Act — first state-level transaction tax on crypto activity
items.idea: Illinois Governor JB Pritzker signed the Digital Asset Tax Act into law as part of the state's budget bill — creating what appears to be the first state-level transaction tax on digital asset activity in the US. This happened while federal crypto legislation remains unresolved. The state-level action fills the regulatory vacuum with a revenue motive rather than an investor-protection motive.
items.angle: This is the template watch. If Illinois' crypto transaction tax survives legal challenge and generates revenue, every state with a budget gap will have the same playbook within 12-18 months. Federal clarity's delay created the opening for state-level fragmentation. For exchanges and DeFi protocols, this is a new compliance layer per state.
items.tweetA: Illinois just signed the first state-level transaction tax on digital asset activity — part of the state budget bill. This is what happens when federal crypto legislation takes years and state budgets have holes. Every state treasurer with a deficit is watching this. The fragmentation starts now.
items.tweetB: A state digital asset transaction tax. Not investor protection. Not AML. Just: we need revenue and crypto is liquid. Federal delay created the opening. If this survives challenge, 10-15 other states with budget gaps follow within a year. Every exchange and DeFi protocol operating in US states just got a new compliance problem.

items.title: First confidential yield vault on Ethereum using fully homomorphic encryption — the privacy primitive DeFi institutions need
items.idea: Zama, Morpho, and Steakhouse Financial are teaming up to launch the first confidential yield vault on Ethereum, using fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) to let users earn yield on USDC without exposing their activities onchain. FHE allows computation on encrypted data — meaning positions and amounts stay private while still being verifiable. This solves the core objection institutional DeFi users have had: all transactions are public.
items.angle: FHE on Ethereum for yield products is the privacy primitive that institutional DeFi adoption has been waiting for. Watch adoption rate on this specific vault — if institutions move stablecoin yield flows here, the entire DeFi institutional narrative changes. The 'all transactions public' critique has a working answer now.
items.tweetA: First confidential yield vault on Ethereum: Zama + Morpho + Steakhouse using fully homomorphic encryption. Earn yield on USDC without exposing your positions or amounts onchain. The 'all DeFi is public' objection that killed institutional adoption just got its first real answer. Watch the FHE adoption curve from here.
items.tweetB: FHE lets you compute on encrypted data. Zama just shipped it for DeFi yield. You can now earn on USDC on Ethereum while keeping your position and amount encrypted. This is the privacy primitive institutional money was waiting for before entering DeFi seriously. The technology works. Now watch whether institutions follow.
